locked_parameters.observable_distribution: P is the observable law of a two-period continuous-treatment DiD array O=(Y_0,Y_1,D), with D taking values on a fixed ordered finite grid \mathcal D=\{d_0<d_1<\cdots<d_K\}, where d_0=0 denotes the untreated or baseline dose.
locked_parameters.parameter_theta: The latent causal array is \alpha_{j h}=\operatorname{ATT}(d_j\mid d_h)=\E[Y_1(d_j)-Y_1(d_0)\mid D=d_h]. The diagonal entries \alpha_{h h}, the definitional baseline row \alpha_{0h}=0, and the Callaway--Goodman-Bacon--Sant'Anna observed level-change contrasts \tau_h(P)=\E[Y_1-Y_0\mid D=d_h]-\E[Y_1-Y_0\mid D=d_0] are taken as the identified inputs under generalized parallel trends. The target is the finite-grid average causal response on the treated at dose d_h, \theta_h=\operatorname{ACRT}(d_h)=\sum_{j=0}^K q_{j h}\alpha_{j h}, where q_{\cdot h} is a locked local finite-difference stencil satisfying \sum_jq_{j h}=0 and \sum_jq_{j h}(d_j-d_h)=1.
locked_parameters.identifying_restrictions: Consistency, no anticipation, generalized parallel trends for the diagonal treated-type parameters, bounded outcomes \alpha_{j h}\in[\underline y,\overline y], and either the row-wise structural Lipschitz restriction |\alpha_{j h}-\alpha_{j h'}|\le L|d_h-d_{h'}| for all j,h,h', or the row-wise isotonic restriction in the conditioning group d_h.
locked_parameters.bounding_functional: The algebraic finite-grid envelope [L_h(L;P),U_h(L;P)], the image of the joint feasible nuisance polytope under the linear map \alpha\mapsto\theta_h, together with the critical regularity constant L_h^\star(P)=\inf\{L:0\in[L_h(L;P),U_h(L;P)]\}. Law sharpness of this algebraic envelope as a causal interval is the separate completion claim in Conjecture 1.

observable_mean_notation.completion_identity: \E_Q[Y_1(d_j)\mid D=d_h]=\beta_h(P)+\alpha_{jh}(Q) for j\neq h, because Y_1(d_h)=Y_1 and \alpha_{hh}(Q)=\tau_h(P).

named_assumptions.label: ass:grid-bounds
named_assumptions.statement: The dose grid \mathcal D=\{d_0<\cdots<d_K\} is fixed, each dose group has positive probability, and every level treatment effect satisfies \alpha_{j h}\in[\underline y,\overline y]. The same compact interval is also used as the support bound for the off-arm completion kernels introduced in Assumption~\ref{ass:completion}. This keeps both the algebraic polytope and the completion-compatible mean restrictions finite-dimensional.

named_assumptions.label: ass:consistency-noanticip
named_assumptions.statement: Observed outcomes satisfy Y_1=Y_1(D), and the pre-period outcome is not affected by the later dose. This is the continuous-treatment DiD convention used by \cite{CallawayGoodmanBaconSantAnna2021}.

named_assumptions.label: ass:cgs-inputs
named_assumptions.statement: The generalized parallel-trends assumptions of \cite{CallawayGoodmanBaconSantAnna2021} identify \tau_h(P)=\E[Y_1-Y_0\mid D=d_h]-\E[Y_1-Y_0\mid D=d_0] and hence the diagonal treated-type effects \alpha_{hh}=\tau_h(P). Together with the definitional baseline rows \alpha_{0h}=0, these are exactly the rows in A_{\mathrm{CGS}}\alpha=b_{\mathrm{CGS}}(P). This assumption imports the identified CGS inputs but does not impose strong parallel trends or set cross-dose selection bias to zero.

named_assumptions.label: ass:regularity
named_assumptions.statement: For the Lipschitz version, a finite L\in[0,\bar L] is fixed and each treatment row h\mapsto\alpha_{j h} obeys |\alpha_{j h}-\alpha_{j h'}|\le L|d_h-d_{h'}|. For the isotonic version, each row is monotone in the conditioning group. These are structural cross-dose variants of the monotone and smoothness restrictions used in \cite{ManskiPepper2000} and \cite{ArmstrongKolesar2018}.

named_assumptions.label: ass:completion
named_assumptions.statement: For the sharpness question, a latent law Q preserves the full observed law P, satisfies consistency and no anticipation, and sets Y_1(d_h)=Y_1 almost surely on each observed cell \{D=d_h\}. Conditional on (Y_0,Y_1,D=d_h), the off-arm vector (Y_1(d_j))_{j\neq h} may be assigned by a finite product kernel supported on [\underline y,\overline y]^K. Thus the completion argument discretizes only the unobserved off-arm coordinates and does not impose finite support on the observed on-arm outcome Y_1. Consequently every realizable array obeys \underline y\le \beta_h(P)+\alpha_{jh}(Q)\le\overline y for all h and all j\neq h.

named_assumptions.label: ass:sampling-lp
named_assumptions.statement: The sample is iid across units; the empirical CGS input vector \widehat b_{\mathrm{CGS}} is asymptotically linear and root-n normal; the population LPs are feasible and finite; and on each regular active region the endpoint value has a nonempty compact dual solution set. This is the finite-dimensional LP setup used in \cite{Voronin2025} and the projection-inference setup of \cite{KaidoMolinariStoye2019}.

named_assumptions.label: ass:frontier-contact
named_assumptions.statement: For inference on L_h^\star(P), the zero-contact set is either a singleton with nonzero one-sided slope in L, a finite tie set with separated candidate slopes, or a compact flat face for which the support-function directional derivative exists. This is the standard nonsmooth-contact taxonomy used for frontier and interval inversion.

estimand_functional: \Theta_h^{\mathrm{alg}}(L;P)=[L_h(L;P),U_h(L;P)] with endpoints over \mathcal A_L(P), and \Theta_h^{\mathrm{mc}}(L;P)=[L_h^{\mathrm{mc}}(L;P),U_h^{\mathrm{mc}}(L;P)] with endpoints over \mathcal A_L^{\mathrm{mc}}(P)=\{\alpha\in\mathcal A_L(P): \underline y\le \beta_h(P)+\alpha_{jh}\le\overline y\ \forall h,\ \forall j\neq h\}; by construction \Theta_h^{\mathrm{mc}}(L;P)\subseteq\Theta_h^{\mathrm{alg}}(L;P).
completion_compatible_set: \mathcal A_L^{\mathrm{mc}}(P)=\{\alpha\in\mathcal A_L(P): \underline y\le \beta_h(P)+\alpha_{jh}\le \overline y\ \forall h,\ \forall j\neq h\}, with \mathcal A_{\mathrm{iso}}^{\mathrm{mc}}(P) defined analogously from \mathcal A_{\mathrm{iso}}(P).

conjectures.label: law-sharp
conjectures.statement: Under Assumptions~\ref{ass:grid-bounds}--\ref{ass:completion}, write \(\beta_h(P)=\mu_{1h}(P)-\tau_h(P)\) and define \(\mathcal A_L^{\mathrm{mc}}(P)=\{\alpha\in\mathcal A_L(P): \underline y\le \beta_h(P)+\alpha_{jh}\le \overline y\ \forall h,\ \forall j\neq h\}.\) Then the mean-completion envelope \([L_h^{\mathrm{mc}}(L;P),U_h^{\mathrm{mc}}(L;P)] = \{\theta_h(\alpha):\alpha\in\mathcal A_L^{\mathrm{mc}}(P)\} = \Theta_h^{\mathrm{mc}}(L;P)\) is law-sharp: \[[L_h^{\mathrm{mc}}(L;P),U_h^{\mathrm{mc}}(L;P)] = \{\operatorname{ACRT}(d_h;Q):Q \text{ is a finite bounded latent law preserving } P \text{ and Assumptions } \ref{ass:consistency-noanticip} \text{--}\ref{ass:regularity}\}.\] In particular, \([L_h(L;P),U_h(L;P)]\) remains an outer algebraic bound, while no endpoint of the corrected mean-completion interval is an outer-bound artifact caused by an unrealizable cross-dose ATT array.

method_exemplar.carried_over: Observational-equivalence discipline separating algebraic feasibility from latent-law realization when assessing sharp identified sets.

conjectures.label: critical-l
conjectures.statement: Under Assumptions~\ref{ass:grid-bounds}--\ref{ass:regularity}, the endpoint functions \(L\mapsto L_h(L;P)\) and \(L\mapsto U_h(L;P)\) are monotone piecewise linear on every finite grid. The critical constant \[L_h^\star(P)=\inf\{L\in[0,\bar L]:L_h(L;P)\le0\le U_h(L;P)\}\] is equivalently the optimum of a finite parametric LP feasibility problem in \((\alpha,L)\): \[ \min_{\alpha,L}\ L \quad\text{subject to}\quad A_{\mathrm{CGS}}\alpha=b_{\mathrm{CGS}}(P),\ \theta_h(\alpha)=0,\ \underline y\le\alpha_{jh}\le\overline y,\ |\alpha_{j h}-\alpha_{j h'}|\le L|d_h-d_{h'}|,\ 0\le L\le\bar L . \] For each nonsingular active basis of this LP, \(L_h^\star(P)\) is an affine function of \(b_{\mathrm{CGS}}(P)\) on the region where that basis remains optimal; globally it is the lower envelope of these basis-specific affine pieces.

method_exemplar.carried_over: Treating the bound as an optimized object and inverting the optimized interval in the regularity radius L to obtain a sensitivity frontier.

conjectures.label: plugin-inference
conjectures.statement: Under Assumptions~\ref{ass:sampling-lp}--\ref{ass:frontier-contact}, define \(\widehat L_h(L)\), \(\widehat U_h(L)\), and \(\widehat L_h^\star\) by replacing \(b_{\mathrm{CGS}}(P)\) with an asymptotically linear estimate and relaxing equality and inequality constraints by calibrated Gaussian or bootstrap critical values. Then the resulting confidence set covers the algebraic envelope endpoints \([L_h(L;P),U_h(L;P)]\) and the critical constant \(L_h^\star(P)\) pointwise, and remains nonempty with probability approaching one even when the unrelaxed sample LP is infeasible. If Conjecture~\ref{conj:law-sharp} holds, the same endpoint confidence set covers the corrected law-sharp ACRT interval through \([L_h^{\mathrm{mc}}(L;P),U_h^{\mathrm{mc}}(L;P)]\subseteq[L_h(L;P),U_h(L;P)]\subseteq[\widehat L_h(L),\widehat U_h(L)]\), or after replacing the sample LP by the analogous relaxed mean-completion program.

method_exemplar.carried_over: Relaxed projection inference for partially identified functionals by optimizing over sample analog constraints widened by calibrated critical values.

supporting_theorems.label: joint-envelope
supporting_theorems.statement: Under Assumptions~\ref{ass:grid-bounds}--\ref{ass:regularity}, if \(\mathcal A_L(P)\) is nonempty, then the finite-grid ACRT algebraic envelope under the cross-dose restrictions is \[ \Theta_h^{\mathrm{alg}}(L;P)= [L_h(L;P),U_h(L;P)] = \{\theta_h(\alpha):\alpha\in\mathcal A_L(P)\}. \] The endpoints are attained by finite linear programs. Equivalently, \[ U_h(L;P)=\sigma_{\mathcal A_L(P)}(q_h\otimes e_h), \qquad L_h(L;P)=-\sigma_{\mathcal A_L(P)}(-q_h\otimes e_h), \] where \(\sigma_C(v)=\sup_{x\in C}v^\transpose x\) is the support function. The same statement holds for the isotonic set \(\mathcal A_{\mathrm{iso}}(P)\).
supporting_theorems.proof_tex:
\begin{theorem}[Theorem 1: joint polytope support envelope]\label{thm:joint-envelope}
Under Assumptions~\ref{ass:grid-bounds}--\ref{ass:regularity}, if \(\mathcal A_L(P)\) is nonempty, then the finite-grid ACRT algebraic envelope under the cross-dose restrictions is
\[
  \Theta_h^{\mathrm{alg}}(L;P)=
  [L_h(L;P),U_h(L;P)]
  =
  \{\theta_h(\alpha):\alpha\in\mathcal A_L(P)\}.
\]
The endpoints are attained by finite linear programs.  Equivalently,
\[
  U_h(L;P)=\sigma_{\mathcal A_L(P)}(q_h\otimes e_h),
  \qquad
  L_h(L;P)=-\sigma_{\mathcal A_L(P)}(-q_h\otimes e_h),
\]
where \(\sigma_C(v)=\sup_{x\in C}v^\transpose x\) is the support function.
The same statement holds for the isotonic set
\(\mathcal A_{\mathrm{iso}}(P)\).
\end{theorem}
\begin{proof}
Work in \(\R^{(K+1)^2}\) after stacking \(\alpha\) by conditioning-dose blocks, so the block indexed by \(h\) is \((\alpha_{0h},\ldots,\alpha_{Kh})\).  Under Assumption~\ref{ass:grid-bounds}, the box restriction \([\underline y,\overline y]^{(K+1)^2}\) is compact.  The equations \(A_{\mathrm{CGS}}\alpha=b_{\mathrm{CGS}}(P)\) are affine, and the Lipschitz inequalities \(|\alpha_{jh}-\alpha_{jh'}|\le L|d_h-d_{h'}|\) can be written as the finite family of linear inequalities \(\alpha_{jh}-\alpha_{jh'}\le L|d_h-d_{h'}|\) and \(\alpha_{jh'}-\alpha_{jh}\le L|d_h-d_{h'}|\).  Hence \(\mathcal A_L(P)\) is the intersection of a compact box with finitely many closed half-spaces and an affine subspace.  If it is nonempty, then it is a nonempty compact convex polytope.

The map \(\theta_h(\alpha)=\sum_{j=0}^K q_{jh}\alpha_{jh}\) is linear and therefore continuous.  By compactness, the extrema
\[
  L_h(L;P)=\min_{\alpha\in\mathcal A_L(P)}\theta_h(\alpha),
  \qquad
  U_h(L;P)=\max_{\alpha\in\mathcal A_L(P)}\theta_h(\alpha)
\]
are attained.  Because both the objective and the feasible set are finite-dimensional and polyhedral, each endpoint is the value of a finite linear program.

It remains to identify the image of \(\mathcal A_L(P)\) under \(\theta_h\).  Since \(\mathcal A_L(P)\) is convex and \(\theta_h\) is linear, the image \(\theta_h(\mathcal A_L(P))\subset\R\) is convex.  Every convex subset of \(\R\) is an interval, and compactness makes it a compact interval.  Its lower and upper endpoints are exactly the attained minimum and maximum above.  Therefore
\[
  \theta_h(\mathcal A_L(P))=[L_h(L;P),U_h(L;P)],
\]
which is the desired set equality.

For the support-function representation, let \(e_h\) be the \((K+1)\)-vector selecting the block indexed by \(h\).  Under the chosen stacking convention, the coefficient vector for \(\theta_h\) is \(q_h\otimes e_h\), so for every feasible \(\alpha\),
\[
  \theta_h(\alpha)=(q_h\otimes e_h)^\transpose \alpha.
\]
Hence
\[
  U_h(L;P)=\sup_{\alpha\in\mathcal A_L(P)}(q_h\otimes e_h)^\transpose\alpha
  =\sigma_{\mathcal A_L(P)}(q_h\otimes e_h).
\]
Applying the same identity to the linear functional \(-\theta_h\) gives
\[
  -L_h(L;P)=\sup_{\alpha\in\mathcal A_L(P)}-(q_h\otimes e_h)^\transpose\alpha
  =\sigma_{\mathcal A_L(P)}(-q_h\otimes e_h),
\]
which rearranges to
\[
  L_h(L;P)=-\sigma_{\mathcal A_L(P)}(-q_h\otimes e_h).
\]
For the isotonic alternative, one replaces the Lipschitz inequalities by the finite linear order restrictions \(\alpha_{j0}\le\alpha_{j1}\le\cdots\le\alpha_{jK}\) (or their reversed counterparts).  The feasible set \(\mathcal A_{\mathrm{iso}}(P)\) is again a nonempty compact convex polytope, and the same compactness, convexity, LP-attainment, and support-function arguments apply verbatim.
\end{proof}

substrate_survey.reason: No directly reusable local primitive surfaced in the name-only survey; this Stage 0.0 artifact is a TeX setup repair, not a Lean construction.
The current repair concerns notation and completion-law typing, so no lower-level Lean substrate reuse is needed at this stage.
